content.text: Runtime Upgrade Testing and Promotion Gates

Promoting a new runtime version into a GenAI production environment requires more than running a test suite. A runtime version change — Python minor version, PyTorch major version, CUDA toolkit upgrade — affects inference accuracy, throughput, GPU memory usage, and failure modes in ways that only surface under real traffic patterns. The promotion gate model structures this risk: each gate is a checkpoint with defined pass/fail criteria that must be satisfied before the version advances to the next environment.
The four-gate model covers the full journey from Evaluation to Production, with automatic rollback at each gate if criteria are not met.

attrs.alt: Runtime version promotion pipeline: Evaluation → Gate 1 → Staging → Gate 2 → Canary → Gate 3 → Production → Gate 4 post-production watch, with automatic rollback on gate failure

content.text: Gate 1 is fully automated and runs in CI as part of the version submission workflow. It validates that the candidate version passes the basic compatibility and security requirements before it is deployed to any shared environment.
100% pass rate — no failures or skips
Block; report test names to submitter
All stack combination smoke tests pass
No CRITICAL or HIGH CVEs without mitigation
Block; link CVE report to submitter
No import errors on the full dependency set
Logits on reference inputs within 1e-4 tolerance of baseline

# .github/workflows/gate1-eval-to-staging.yml
name: Gate 1 — Evaluation to Staging

on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      candidate:
        description: "Package==version to evaluate (e.g. torch==2.5.0)"
        required: true

jobs:
  gate1:
    runs-on: [self-hosted, gpu-eval]
    timeout-minutes: 60
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Install candidate version
        run: pip install ${{ github.event.inputs.candidate }}

      - name: Unit tests
        run: pytest tests/unit/ -v --tb=short --junit-xml=results/unit.xml

      - name: Compatibility smoke tests
        run: python scripts/stack_compat_smoke.py --candidate "${{ github.event.inputs.candidate }}"

      - name: CVE scan
        run: |
          pip-audit --format=json --output=results/cve.json
          python scripts/check_cve_severity.py results/cve.json --fail-on HIGH,CRITICAL

      - name: Numerical precision check
        run: python scripts/precision_check.py --baseline baselines/logits_reference.json --tolerance 1e-4

      - name: Record Gate 1 result
        if: success()
        run: |
          python scripts/record_gate_pass.py \
            --gate 1 \
            --candidate "${{ github.event.inputs.candidate }}" \
            --ci-run "${{ github.run_id }}"

Gate 2 runs against the staging environment after a full deployment of the candidate version to staging infrastructure. Unlike Gate 1 (smoke tests in CI), Gate 2 exercises the candidate version under full workload conditions using the production model weights and a replayed traffic sample.
The staging benchmark compares the candidate version against the currently approved baseline on three dimensions:
Runtime version upgrades should not regress throughput
Tail latency allows more headroom for JIT compilation variance
Memory regressions reduce the number of concurrent requests
Pod startup time affects scaling latency

The regression suite runs the complete golden prompt set and compares outputs against the approved baseline using both semantic equivalence (embedding cosine similarity) and safety classification consistency.
Performance thresholds exist because framework upgrades frequently change memory allocation patterns, JIT compilation behaviour, and kernel selection.
A PyTorch 2.4 → 2.5 upgrade might select different CUDA kernels that improve some workloads and regress others. The staging benchmark surfaces these regressions before live users are affected.

Gate 3 controls the transition from the canary deployment (typically 5% of production traffic) to the full production rollout. Unlike Gates 1 and 2, Gate 3 is primarily time-based: the canary must run stably for 24 hours under live traffic before promotion proceeds.

# kubernetes/canary-deployment.yaml
# Deploys the candidate runtime version to 5% of inference pods
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: inference-canary
  labels:
    version-track: canary
    candidate-version: "torch-2.5.0"
spec:
  replicas: 2   # 2 of 40 total pods = 5%
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: inference-service
      version-track: canary
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: inference-service
        version-track: canary
      annotations:
        version.policy/candidate: "torch==2.5.0"
        version.policy/gate: "3"
        version.policy/canary-start: "2025-10-15T09:00:00Z"
    spec:
      containers:
        - name: inference
          image: inference-service:canary-torch250
          env:
            - name: INFERENCE_VERSION_TRACK
              value: canary
            - name: EMIT_VERSION_TAG
              value: "true"   # tags all metrics with version-track label

The canary monitoring job runs every 5 minutes and compares key signals between the canary pods and the stable baseline pods. An automatic rollback is triggered if any signal exceeds the rollback threshold:
Canary error rate > 2× stable error rate
Any OOM in canary that is absent in stable
Any canary output classified differently to baseline

# scripts/canary_monitor.py
# Runs every 5 minutes via cron; triggers rollback if thresholds are exceeded
import requests, os, subprocess, sys

PROMETHEUS = os.environ["PROMETHEUS_URL"]
ROLLBACK_SCRIPT = "scripts/rollback_canary.sh"

def query(promql: str) -> float:
    resp = requests.get(f"{PROMETHEUS}/api/v1/query",
                        params={"query": promql}, timeout=10)
    data = resp.json()["data"]["result"]
    return float(data[0]["value"][1]) if data else 0.0

def check_canary_health() -> list[str]:
    violations = []

    canary_err = query('rate(http_requests_total{version_track="canary",status=~"5.."}[5m])')
    stable_err = query('rate(http_requests_total{version_track="stable",status=~"5.."}[5m])')
    if stable_err > 0 and canary_err > 2 * stable_err:
        violations.append(f"Error rate: canary={canary_err:.4f} > 2x stable={stable_err:.4f}")

    canary_p99 = query('histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(inference_latency_seconds_bucket{version_track="canary"}[15m]))')
    stable_p99 = query('histogram_quantile(0.99, rate(inference_latency_seconds_bucket{version_track="stable"}[15m]))')
    if stable_p99 > 0 and canary_p99 > stable_p99 * 1.25:
        violations.append(f"p99 latency: canary={canary_p99:.3f}s > 1.25x stable={stable_p99:.3f}s")

    gpu_oom = query('increase(gpu_oom_events_total{version_track="canary"}[5m])')
    if gpu_oom > 0:
        violations.append(f"GPU OOM: {gpu_oom:.0f} OOM events in canary (none expected)")

    return violations

def rollback():
    subprocess.run([ROLLBACK_SCRIPT], check=True)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    violations = check_canary_health()
    if violations:
        print("CANARY ROLLBACK TRIGGERED:")
        for v in violations:
            print(f"  ✗ {v}")
        rollback()
        sys.exit(1)
    print("Canary health check: OK")

Passing Gate 3 and completing the full rollout is not the end of the promotion process. Runtimes with significant differences in memory management or JIT compilation behavior can degrade gradually over hours or days as model caches grow, memory fragmentation accumulates, or rare code paths are exercised.
Gate 4 is a 7-day post-production watch period with defined thresholds. The version is not considered fully approved until it passes the watch period.
Remains at or below pre-upgrade baseline
No upward trend over 48-hour window (OLS slope < 1%/day)
Driver or CUDA instability suspected; escalate
At the end of the 7-day watch period, the platform team reviews the telemetry dashboard and records the final approval decision:

# scripts/complete_promotion.sh
# Run after 7-day watch period passes all criteria
# Arguments: PACKAGE VERSION BASELINE_RUN_ID

PACKAGE=$1
VERSION=$2
BASELINE_RUN=$3

echo "Completing promotion: ${PACKAGE}==${VERSION}"

# 1. Update version matrix: set this version to Approved, prior to Deprecated
python scripts/update_version_matrix.py \
  --package "${PACKAGE}" \
  --version "${VERSION}" \
  --new-status approved \
  --prior-status deprecated \
  --approval-run "${BASELINE_RUN}"

# 2. Record in audit log
python scripts/audit_log.py record \
  --event "version-approved" \
  --package "${PACKAGE}" \
  --version "${VERSION}" \
  --approver "$(git config user.email)" \
  --watch-period-end "$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)"

# 3. Notify engineering teams
python scripts/notify_teams.py \
  --message "${PACKAGE} ${VERSION} is now Approved for production. Prior version is Deprecated — upgrade by deadline in version-matrix.yaml."

echo "Promotion complete. ${PACKAGE}==${VERSION} is now Approved."
Testing Special Cases in Runtime Upgrades

Standard regression suites test the happy path. Runtime upgrades can break the failure handling paths — exception types change, error messages change, timeout behavior changes — in ways that standard tests miss.
The failure path test suite explicitly tests degraded conditions under the new runtime version:

# tests/compat/failure_paths.py
# Tests that failure handling works correctly under the new runtime version

import pytest, asyncio, torch
from unittest.mock import patch
from app.inference import InferenceService

@pytest.fixture
def service():
    return InferenceService(model_path="models/test-model")

def test_oom_handling_raises_resource_error(service):
    # Simulate OOM: should raise ResourceError, not raw CUDA exception
    # Some PyTorch versions changed the exception type for OOM
    with patch("torch.Tensor.to", side_effect=torch.cuda.OutOfMemoryError("OOM")):
        with pytest.raises(ResourceError, match="GPU memory"):
            service.generate("test prompt", max_tokens=100)

def test_timeout_propagates_correctly(service):
    # asyncio.TimeoutError handling changed in Python 3.11
    # Verify the new runtime propagates it as our InferenceTimeoutError
    async def slow_generate():
        await asyncio.sleep(10)
    with pytest.raises(InferenceTimeoutError):
        asyncio.run(asyncio.wait_for(slow_generate(), timeout=0.1))

def test_model_load_error_handled(service, tmp_path):
    # PyTorch checkpoint loading exceptions changed between 2.x versions
    bad_model_path = str(tmp_path / "nonexistent.pt")
    with pytest.raises(ModelLoadError):
        InferenceService(model_path=bad_model_path)

def test_invalid_dtype_raises_type_error(service):
    # CUDA dtype handling changed in PyTorch 2.4 — verify cast error is caught
    with pytest.raises(TypeError, match="unsupported dtype"):
        service.generate("test", dtype="float96")  # invalid dtype

Runtime version upgrades sometimes affect thread safety, especially for models that use shared state (KV caches, batch queues). The concurrency test fires multiple simultaneous inference requests and verifies there are no race conditions or cross-request contamination:

# tests/compat/concurrency.py
import asyncio, pytest
from app.inference import InferenceService

CONCURRENT_REQUESTS = 50
TEST_PROMPTS = [f"Test prompt {i}" for i in range(CONCURRENT_REQUESTS)]

@pytest.mark.asyncio
async def test_concurrent_inference_no_contamination():
    service = InferenceService(model_path="models/test-model")
    # Fire all requests simultaneously
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        *[service.generate_async(prompt) for prompt in TEST_PROMPTS]
    )
    # Each result should correspond to its prompt — no cross-request contamination
    for i, result in enumerate(results):
        assert f"prompt {i}" in result.context or result.request_id == i, \
            f"Request {i} returned contaminated result"
    # No two results should be identical (would indicate shared state corruption)
    result_texts = [r.text for r in results]
    assert len(set(result_texts)) > len(result_texts) * 0.9, \
        "Suspiciously similar results — possible shared state corruption"

Runtime upgrade testing gates convert a high-risk operation — replacing a core runtime in a production GenAI stack — into a structured, reversible process. The four-gate model provides increasing confidence at each stage: automated compatibility checks in CI (Gate 1), full regression and performance benchmarks in staging (Gate 2), live traffic validation in canary (Gate 3), and a 7-day post-production watch period (Gate 4).

Each gate has defined pass criteria and automatic rollback behavior. The automation is the key: a gate that requires manual sign-off for every metric check creates bottlenecks that teams route around by skipping the process. Gates that enforce themselves through CI pipelines, Kubernetes canary configurations, and Prometheus-triggered rollback scripts become the path of least resistance rather than an obstacle.

The special-case tests — failure path testing, concurrency validation — address the categories of regression that standard regression suites miss. Runtime upgrades break error handling and thread safety in ways that only surface under concurrent load or degraded conditions, not in single-request functional tests. Including these as required gate criteria prevents a class of production incidents that are disproportionately difficult to diagnose after the fact.
